text: raids on coastal villages on all of the isles adjacent to Elleron territory. Aurel read through the list of ships, the names of the villages, his anger escalating again, but his anger cut short when he reached the end. He took a step forward involuntarily and Rickard glanced back at the movement. “Aurel?” Aurel shot a look toward the far end of the table, saw the mirrorwright watching him. He straightened, then placed himself at the edge of table, facing Rickard, back to Ergant and his mirrorwright. Rickard frowned up at him. “You’ve forgotten Istabol,” he muttered, and pointed toward the list of towns and villages that had been raided. “On the isle of Kent, your northernmost holding.” Irritation flashed through Rickard’s eyes and he waved a hand. “I haven’t forgotten it. I can’t list every village affected by these ‘raids’ along our coast. We wouldn’t have enough parchment.” Ergant cleared his throat and both Rickard and Aurel looked toward the far end of the table. The king held up his own sheet of parchment. “If you are finished?” Rickard stood, picked up his own sheet, handed it to Aurel. Aurel held it a moment, frowning, until Rickard gestured for him to move. He scanned the list again as he walked down the side of the table, the foreign mirrorwright mirroring him. The list of villages appeared complete. Perhaps a few ports—no more than landings—missing. Nothing as significant as Istabol. They met near the center of the table, face to face, not even an arm’s-length apart. Aurel sucked in a deep breath, smelled some kind of spice, sharp and enticing. The mirrorwright bowed her head formally. “Utak. It is a pleasure to meet a fellow walker.” It took a moment for him to realize Utak was her name, what with the distraction of the missing village and the peculiarity of her accent. “Aurel,” he mumbled, and nodded in return. She smiled and held out Ergant’s grievances. He hesitated, then took the page, handed over Rickard’s list, and headed back toward his king. Rickard snatched it from his hands, gaze flashing over the neat, elegant script. “You call these grievances? These are nothing but complaints, most of them the travails of doing business in the Isles.” “I could say the same of your own list,” Ergant said, tossing Rickard’s paper to the table. “I questioned your lineage? The war for the throne—which your father won—lasted ten years. Rumors that the bastard line prevailed have been circulating ever since.” “That is all that they are. Rumors.” “And the trade deal with the Southern Winds Company fell through because the cargo was never delivered to port.” “I have papers signed by your harbormaster that say it did. You simply refused to pay.” Ergant leaned forward. “Do you have these papers?” Rickard reached into the fold of his formal jacket and Aurel saw Utak tense. Rickard noticed as well, hand stilled. He withdrew a folded piece of parchment with a contemptuous grunt, held it up in plain view. “Simply paper, nothing more.” Utak eased back and nodded. Aurel passed the document to Utak, as before, and retreated. Ergant unfolded it, inspected it and the seal at the bottom closely. “Assuming this is not a forgery, then an apology is in order.” He handed the paper to Utak. “What about my own grievances? You promised a shipment of iron ingots in return for the use of our masons. We have yet to receive it, even though my masons have already begun work.” Rickard sighed. “The ship carrying that cargo was delayed by weather. You know the waters of the straight are vicious this time of year.” Ergant lips thinned in disbelief. “Delayed by four weeks?” “Damaged in a storm and seeking repairs.” The conversation continued, a dance of parry and thrust, but with words. Ground was given and taken. Aurel listened in growing agitation as trivial matters were settled, concessions made, agreements reached. Parchment passed between his and Utak’s hands, signed and sealed with wax. Yet neither king had broached the real grievance yet—the attacks on the trading ships and islands along their border. At an agreed upon break, Aurel asked, “Why have you not brought up the raids upon our villages?” On Istabol, he wanted to say. The raids that had killed Elean and her family, left them butchered, blood soaking into the sands, as the fishermen’s huts burned. “Soon. That is practically all that is left to discuss.” Rickard ripped a few grapes from a clutch and popped them into his mouth as Aurel poured them both a glass of wine. The king’s eyes never left Ergant, narrowed in hunger, as he stabbed at the sliced meat, sopping up juices with bread. “He will not be able to dance his way free of our accusations with words. Reparations will be needed. Tregard will walk away with his southern isles.” Aurel frowned. “What about reparations to those that remain in the villages that were attacked? They lost their homes, their livelihood…loved ones.” Rickard glanced up at him in annoyance. “Of course. That’s what I meant when I said reparations. I haven’t forgotten Istabol. Nor what you lost.” Elean. His wife. Visiting her family, simple fisherfolk, minding their own business, plying the sea, unaware of all of the political maneuvering surrounding their island and the waterways near it. Yet they’d paid the price. Aurel’s fingers tightened on the stem of his glass and he was forced to set it down. He wiped his sweaty hand against his jacket and realized Rickard was watching him closely. “You should try some of the cheese,” his king said. “It’s rather good.” “I’m not hungry.” “Understandable after what happened to your wife.” Before Aurel could respond, Rickard’s gaze flashed toward Ergant’s end of the table. “It would appear we are to begin again.” He stood, Aurel stepping to the side and behind him again. At the far end of the table, Ergant had shoved the tray of food aside.
